title: COCO: Objects in Context
concept: COCO (Common Objects in Context) is a benchmark dataset designed for object detection, segmentation, and captioning.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does top-5 error rate measure in classification?

options.text: Whether the ground-truth label is within the model's top 5 highest-confidence guesses
The error rate of the top 5 worst-performing classes in the dataset
The percentage of images containing at least 5 objects

exercises.explain: Top-5 error checks if the correct category falls anywhere in the top 5 predicted probabilities.

cards.title: Finding a cup in a kitchen
cards.body: In the real world, objects don't appear in isolation on clean white backgrounds. A cup is usually on a table next to a plate. To train models that understand real scenes, we need images showing everyday objects in their natural environments.

cards.title: COCO Dataset
cards.body: COCO (Common Objects in Context) is a large-scale dataset featuring over 328,000 images labeled with bounding boxes, segmentation masks, keypoints, and natural captions for 80 everyday categories.

cards.title: Spotting Waldo in a Crowd
cards.body: ImageNet is like card cataloging objects on a shelf. COCO is like 'Where's Waldo?' — it requires the model to locate and segment multiple cluttered, overlapping objects within complex backgrounds.

cards.title: Multi-Task Annotations
cards.body: For an image of a street corner, COCO annotations will provide: bounding boxes for cars (detection), polygon outlines for pedestrians (instance segmentation), and keypoints for bicyclist joints (pose estimation).

cards.title: Not just for classification
cards.body: You might think COCO is just a competitor to ImageNet. In reality, COCO is rarely used for simple whole-image classification; it is the gold standard benchmark for localization, detection, and scene parsing.

cards.title: Classification vs. Contextual Scene
cards.body: Comparing the training objectives of ImageNet and COCO:

right.text:
Multiple objects in scene.
Labels: Pedestrian, Car, Stop sign.
Focuses on localization & context.

exercises.explain: COCO stands for Common Objects in Context, focusing on everyday items in their natural scenes.

exercises.prompt: True or False: COCO images only contain a single object per image.
exercises.explain: False. COCO is specifically designed to contain multiple objects in complex, natural layouts.
Segmentation masks in COCO are represented by polygon points tracing the outline of each object.
exercises.sentence: Unlike ImageNet, COCO annotations include polygon coordinates representing segmentation ___.

exercises.prompt: Which task is commonly benchmarked using the COCO dataset?
exercises.explain: COCO is the primary benchmark for object detection, keypoint detection, and instance segmentation.

exercises.prompt: Why are coco evaluation metrics like Average Precision (AP) calculated across multiple Intersection over Union (IoU) thresholds?

options.text: To measure how robust the model's bounding box precision is, from loose overlap (IoU=0.50) to very tight, precise overlap (IoU=0.95).
To speed up the evaluation code running on the CPU.
To determine if the image files were loaded in grayscale.
To balance the class representation in the training batch.

exercises.explain: Averaging AP across multiple IoU thresholds (0.50 to 0.95) penalizes sloppy bounding boxes and rewards pixel-perfect localization.
